fuzzy_prd: hey team, we've been getting complaints from a few enterprise clients that the login flow occasionally breaks in weird ways — tokens seem to get accepted even when they shouldn't, or the redirect after logout lands on the wrong page. I think it's related to how we handle the full sign-in/sign-out cycle but I'm not 100% sure where the gap is. We need to build out a proper client-side toolkit that handles all the plumbing around that OIDC login stuff — building the URLs, reading back what the provider sends us, and making sure the token we get back is actually legit before we let anyone in. The main thing I care about is that if something's wrong with the token, we know *exactly* what went wrong rather than just getting a generic failure. Also there was that discussion last sprint about the SSR environment issue — where certain features were silently staying on when they shouldn't be depending on where the app is running — we need to make sure that's handled too, same logic as the browser-vs-server check we sorted out on the last login module. Code should be properly split up, not one giant file, since the last time we did something like this it became a nightmare to maintain. Can someone pick this up?

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: validateResponse(config, localState, localNonce, responseState, accessToken, idToken, signingKeys, clock): ValidationVerdict

trigger_keywords: what order are the validation checks run
validation pipeline sequence
which check comes first in token validation
what is the order of the token verification steps
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The validation pipeline must run in this exact order: (1) returned state matches locally stored state, (2) identity-token signature verifies against issuer key, (3) token nonce matches locally stored nonce, (4) required claims iss/sub/aud/exp/iat are all present, (5) issue time is within the allowed clock offset, (6) issuer matches configured issuer, (7) audience matches configured client id, (8) token is not expired, (9) access-token hash matches supplied access token when response type requests both tokens. The pipeline stops and reports the first failing stage.

trigger_keywords: what outcome codes are returned
what are the possible result values
list of failure codes
what does the result field contain
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The outcome codes are exactly: StatesDoNotMatch, SignatureFailed, IncorrectNonce, RequiredPropertyMissing, MaxOffsetExpired, IssDoesNotMatchIssuer, IncorrectAud, TokenExpired, IncorrectAtHash, Ok. These are the only valid values for the result field.

trigger_keywords: what does the output format look like for response validation
how many lines does validation output
what fields are in the validation response
what is printed when validation completes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The validation output is exactly five lines in this order: 'result=<stage outcome code>', 'valid=<true|false>', 'access_token=<echoed access token or empty>', 'id_token=<present|empty>', 'decoded_sub=<subject claim or empty>'. When a stage fails before the token is parsed, access_token, id_token, and decoded_sub may be empty strings.

trigger_keywords: browser vs server feature flags
how does SSR affect silent renew
when are silent renew and checksession disabled
platform gating logic for features
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: On an interactive browser platform the silent_renew and start_checksession flags are honored exactly as configured. On a non-interactive server-side rendering platform both features are forced to false regardless of what was requested, because they depend on a live browser environment. Output is two lines: 'silent_renew=<true|false>' and 'start_checksession=<true|false>'.

trigger_keywords: how should the authorization URL be assembled
what order do query parameters appear in the auth URL
how are custom parameters appended
what encoding rules apply to the authorize URL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The authorization URL preserves any query already on the endpoint, then appends client_id, redirect_uri, response_type, scope, nonce, and state in that fixed order, followed by any extra custom request parameters in the order supplied. All keys and values are percent-encoded per application/x-www-form-urlencoded rules: spaces become %20, reserved characters like ;,/?:@&=+$# are escaped, and unreserved characters -_.!~*() are left intact.

trigger_keywords: how is the callback fragment parsed
what happens if the value contains equals signs
how are base64 tokens with trailing == handled in the fragment
splitting logic for redirect fragment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The fragment is split on '&' between pairs and on the first '=' only within each pair. Any additional '=' characters remain inside the value. This means a base64 token ending in '==' or a state value containing '=' is preserved intact. Output prints one key=value line per parsed entry in encounter order.

trigger_keywords: how does loose value equality work
comparing a string against an array
what happens when null is compared
empty string equality behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two strings are equal only when byte-for-byte identical (case sensitive). Two arrays are equal only when they have the same length and identical ordered elements. Two objects are equal when their case-insensitive serialized forms match. A plain string compared against a one-element array is equal when the string equals that single element (in either argument order). Any null or undefined operand forces not-equal. Two empty strings are deliberately treated as not-equal.

trigger_keywords: how should the code be structured
what files or modules are required
is a single file acceptable
code organization requirements
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The implementation must not be a single god file. Required separation includes at minimum: a config layer, a token-decoding helper, a low-level claim-validator, a high-level validation pipeline, a request/URL service, and a test adapter kept apart from the domain. The JSON input/output test adapter must be the only unit that reads stdin and writes stdout; the core logic must have no stdin/stdout or JSON-command coupling.

trigger_keywords: what is the end session URL format
how is the logout URL built
what parameters go in the logout URL
order of parameters in end session request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The end-session URL preserves any query already on the endpoint, then appends id_token_hint followed by post_logout_redirect_uri in that fixed order. Both are percent-encoded per application/x-www-form-urlencoded rules. Output is a single line 'url=<full url>'.

trigger_keywords: how is JWT segment decoding handled for invalid tokens
what happens when the token has wrong number of parts
decoding a token with no dots
null or empty token segment decode result
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any token input that is null, empty, contains no dot, or does not split into exactly three parts is invalid and yields the empty object {}. When encoded is true the raw segment text is returned verbatim. When encoded is false the segment is base64url-decoded and parsed as JSON with UTF-8 decoding applied. Output is a single line 'value=<rendering>' where strings render as-is and objects render as compact JSON.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Here are the public test cases for feature6_response_validation:
{
  "op": "response_validation",
  "description": "Validate a full authorization response (an access token plus a signed identity token) against the client configuration and the issuer signing keys, running the ordered chain of identity-token checks and reporting the first failing stage. The pipeline, in order, is: (1) the returned state must equal the locally stored state; (2) the identity-token signature must verify against the issuer key; (3) the token nonce must equal the locally stored nonce; (4) the required claims iss/sub/aud/exp/iat must all be present; (5) the issue time must be within the allowed offset of now; (6) the issuer must match the configured issuer; (7) the audience must match the configured client id; (8) the token must not be expired; (9) only when the response type requests both an access token and an identity token, the token's access-token hash must match the supplied access token and an access token must be present. When every stage passes the result is the success code and the response is valid. Inputs describe the scenario declaratively (which stored values, which token claims, whether to sign with the correct key, etc.); the adapter mints a real signed token accordingly. Output is five lines: 'result=<stage outcome code>', 'valid=<true|false>', 'access_token=<echoed access token or empty>', 'id_token=<present|empty>', and 'decoded_sub=<subject claim or empty>'. The outcome codes are: StatesDoNotMatch, SignatureFailed, IncorrectNonce, RequiredPropertyMissing, MaxOffsetExpired, IssDoesNotMatchIssuer, IncorrectAud, TokenExpired, IncorrectAtHash, Ok.",

    {
      "input": {
        "client_id": "singleapp",
        "response_type": "id_token token",
        "max_iat_offset_seconds": 10,
        "issuer": "https://localhost:44363",
        "local_state": "st",
        "local_nonce": "nn",
        "response_state": "WRONG",
        "access_token": "AT",
        "id_token_present": false

    {

        "local_nonce": "nn",
        "response_state": "st",
        "access_token": "AT",
        "id_token_present": true,
        "include_at_hash": true,

    {

        "max_iat_offset_seconds": 10,
        "issuer": "https://localhost:44363",
        "local_state": "st",
        "local_nonce": "nn",
        "response_state": "st",

context_pointers.vague_reference: same logic as the browser-vs-server check we sorted out on the last login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7 platform_feature_gating logic in feature7_platform_feature_gating.json: on 'server' platform both silent_renew and start_checksession are forced false regardless of requested config; on 'browser' platform the requested flags are honored as-is.

context_pointers.vague_reference: if something's wrong with the token, we know exactly what went wrong
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 6 response_validation pipeline in feature6_response_validation.json: the result field must contain one of the ten specific outcome codes (StatesDoNotMatch, SignatureFailed, IncorrectNonce, RequiredPropertyMissing, MaxOffsetExpired, IssDoesNotMatchIssuer, IncorrectAud, TokenExpired, IncorrectAtHash, Ok) identifying the first failing stage.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — you'll need to check the existing codebase and test cases in the rcb_tests/public_test_cases directory for the precise behavior expected. The test files for each feature should clarify the exact input/output contract.

feature1_value_equality.json.description: Loose structural equality between two credential-claim values that may each be a string, a string array, an object, or a null/undefined absence. Two strings are equal only when byte-for-byte identical (case sensitive). Two arrays are equal only when they have the same length and identical ordered elements. Two objects are equal when their case-insensitive serialized forms match. A string compared against a single-element array is equal when the string equals that one element. Any null or undefined operand makes the result not-equal, and two empty strings are treated as not-equal. Output is a single line 'equal=true' or 'equal=false'.

feature2_audience_validation.json.description: Verify that the audience embedded in a decoded identity token matches the expected client identifier. When the token audience is a single string, it must exactly equal the expected value. When the token audience is an array of strings, it must equal the expected array element-for-element in order. Output is a single line 'match=true' or 'match=false'.

feature3_1_token_expiration.json.description: Compute the absolute expiration instant of an identity token from its claim set. When the claims contain an 'exp' field (seconds since the Unix epoch, UTC), the output reports 'has_exp=true' followed by 'exp_utc=<ISO-8601 UTC timestamp>' derived from that value. When no 'exp' claim is present, no fixed instant exists; the output reports only 'has_exp=false'.

feature3_2_jwt_segment_decode.json.description: Extract one of the three dot-separated segments (header, payload, or signature) from a compact three-part token string. With 'encoded' true the raw segment text is returned verbatim. With 'encoded' false the segment is base64url-decoded and parsed as JSON, yielding the structured object (UTF-8 / percent-decoding is applied so non-ASCII text is preserved). A token that is null, empty, contains no dot, or does not have exactly three parts is invalid and yields the empty object. Output is a single line 'value=<rendering>' where strings render as-is and objects render as compact JSON.

feature4_1_authorize_url.json.description: Build an authorization request URL from a base authorization endpoint and the client configuration. The endpoint's own pre-existing query (if any) is preserved, then the parameters client_id, redirect_uri, response_type, scope, nonce, and state are appended in that fixed order, followed by any extra custom request parameters in the order supplied. All keys and values are percent-encoded per application/x-www-form-urlencoded rules. Output is a single line 'url=<full url>'.

feature4_2_end_session_url.json.description: Build an end-session (logout) URL from a base end-session endpoint, the identity-token hint, and the configured post-logout redirect URI. The endpoint's pre-existing query (if any) is preserved, then id_token_hint and post_logout_redirect_uri are appended in that fixed order, percent-encoded per application/x-www-form-urlencoded rules. Output is a single line 'url=<full url>'.

feature5_callback_fragment_parse.json.description: Parse a redirect callback fragment of the form 'key1=value1&key2=value2&...' into its key/value map. Splitting is performed on '&' between pairs and on the first '=' within each pair, so any further '=' characters are kept inside the value (e.g. a base64 token ending in '==' or a state value that itself contains '='). Output prints one 'key=value' line per parsed entry in encounter order.

feature6_response_validation.json.description: Validate a full authorization response (an access token plus a signed identity token) against the client configuration and the issuer signing keys, running the ordered chain of identity-token checks and reporting the first failing stage. The pipeline, in order, is: (1) the returned state must equal the locally stored state; (2) the identity-token signature must verify against the issuer key; (3) the token nonce must equal the locally stored nonce; (4) the required claims iss/sub/aud/exp/iat must all be present; (5) the issue time must be within the allowed offset of now; (6) the issuer must match the configured issuer; (7) the audience must match the configured client id; (8) the token must not be expired; (9) only when the response type requests both an access token and an identity token, the token's access-token hash must match the supplied access token and an access token must be present. When every stage passes the result is the success code and the response is valid. Inputs describe the scenario declaratively (which stored values, which token claims, whether to sign with the correct key, etc.); the adapter mints a real signed token accordingly. Output is five lines: 'result=<stage outcome code>', 'valid=<true|false>', 'access_token=<echoed access token or empty>', 'id_token=<present|empty>', and 'decoded_sub=<subject claim or empty>'. The outcome codes are: StatesDoNotMatch, SignatureFailed, IncorrectNonce, RequiredPropertyMissing, MaxOffsetExpired, IssDoesNotMatchIssuer, IncorrectAud, TokenExpired, IncorrectAtHash, Ok.

feature7_platform_feature_gating.json.description: Resolve whether the silent-renew and session-check features are enabled, given the requested configuration flags and the host platform. On an interactive browser platform the requested flags are honored as-is. On a non-interactive (server-side rendering) platform both features are forced off regardless of what was requested, because they depend on a live browser environment. Output is two lines: 'silent_renew=<true|false>' and 'start_checksession=<true|false>'.
